required.title: [Read Aloud / Practice Together] Poems and short speeches for memorization and recitation
[For Mentor]
required.author: Susan Wise Bauer and Jessie Wise

required.title: [For Mentor]
required.author: Douglas M. Fraleigh and Joseph S. Tuman

suggested.title: [Watch Together] Age-appropriate TED-Ed talks or Toastmasters Youth Leadership videos

supplementary.title: [Practice Tool] A recording device (phone or tablet)

competence_markers.description: The student can stand in front of a group of at least six people and deliver a one-minute prepared presentation with clear voice, eye contact, and a beginning, middle, and end
The student can recite a memorized poem or passage with vocal expression, eye contact, and composure
The student can tell a story with clear structure — beginning, middle, and end — and use specific details
The student can answer questions from an audience in complete sentences after a presentation
The student demonstrates comfort being the center of attention in low-stakes settings (leading grace, making announcements, introducing themselves)
The student speaks at sufficient volume to be heard by everyone in the room without being reminded
The student has delivered at least one presentation to an audience beyond their immediate family
The student can watch a recording of themselves speaking and identify one strength and one area for improvement

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you had to speak in front of people.
narration_prompts.detail: What happened? How did you feel? What would you do differently now?

narration_prompts.prompt: Why do you think people get nervous when they have to speak in front of a group?
narration_prompts.detail: What does nervousness feel like in your body? What helps it get better?

narration_prompts.prompt: Recite the poem you have been working on.
narration_prompts.detail: Then tell me: what is the poem about? Why do you think the poet wrote it? What is your favorite line?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me a story about something that happened to you.
narration_prompts.detail: Make sure it has a beginning, a middle, and an end. Use details so I can picture it.

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between talking to one person and talking to a group?
narration_prompts.detail: What do you have to do differently? What stays the same?

narration_prompts.prompt: Who is someone you have seen speak well to a group?
narration_prompts.detail: What did they do that made them good at it? What can you learn from watching them?

cross_connections.description: Your body is the platform for your voice. Eye contact, posture, stillness, and composed gestures are the physical foundation without which public speaking fails. Everything you learned in 3.3.1 is deployed every time you stand up to speak.
Speaking clearly, in complete sentences, at the right volume, is the verbal foundation. Public speaking is verbal communication amplified to a group audience.

Narration — telling back what you learned in your own words — is the bridge between writing and speaking. The student who narrates well after reading is practicing the same organizational skill needed for speeches.
Oral tradition is how civilizations preserved knowledge before writing. When you recite a poem or tell a story aloud, you are participating in the oldest human educational tradition.
A good speaker listens to the audience — watching their faces, noticing their reactions, adjusting. Speaking and listening are two halves of the same skill.
Every time you stand up and speak despite being nervous, you are building resilience. Public speaking is a controlled stress exposure that makes you stronger.

content.why_this_matters:
Here is a fact that will be true for the rest of your life: the person who can stand up and speak clearly in front of other people has power that the person who cannot does not have. This is not about being loud. It is not about being bossy. It is about being able to share what you think, what you know, and what you believe — and having people actually listen.

Right now, you are young enough to build this skill before fear gets in the way. Most adults are terrified of speaking in front of groups. Their hands shake. Their voices go quiet. Their minds go blank. This happens because no one helped them practice when they were your age. By the time school asked them to give a presentation, they were already afraid.

You are going to do something different. You are going to practice standing up and speaking so many times that it feels normal. Not scary. Not special. Just something you do — like reading or riding a bike. And because you start now, you will have an advantage that most people never get: the ability to say what you mean, out loud, in front of others, without being afraid.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Comfort with attention** — the ability to be the focus of a group's attention without freezing, rushing, or shutting down
2. **Basic speech structure** — the habit of organizing what you say with a beginning, a middle, and an end
3. **Vocal projection** — speaking loudly and clearly enough for everyone in the room to hear
4. **Eye contact with an audience** — looking at the people you are speaking to, not at the floor or the ceiling
5. **Recitation and memorization** — the ability to learn and deliver prepared material with confidence
6. **Storytelling as communication** — telling a story with structure, detail, and expression
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Why People Listen When You Stand Up

When you sit in a group and say something, people might hear you. When you stand up and say something, people listen. There is a difference.

Standing changes everything. It tells the room: "I have something to say, and it matters enough for me to stand up and say it." Your body is doing work before your mouth opens. This is why 3.3.1 comes before 3.3.6 — your body has to be ready before your voice can do its job.

Think about the people in your life who are good at speaking to groups. Maybe it is a teacher, a coach, a pastor, or a parent. Watch them. Notice how they stand. Notice where they look. Notice how they use their voice — sometimes louder, sometimes softer, sometimes pausing and saying nothing at all. They are not doing magic. They are using skills. The same skills you are going to learn.

### Part 2: Show and Tell — Your First Speeches

Show and tell is not just a school activity. It is your first real public speaking practice. Here is how to make it count:

**Before you speak:**
- Choose something you genuinely care about or find interesting
- Think about three things you want to say about it: what it is, why you chose it, and something surprising or interesting about it
- That is your speech — beginning (what it is), middle (why you chose it), end (the interesting thing)

**When you speak:**
- Stand up straight. Feet on the floor. Hands at your sides or holding your object — not fidgeting
- Look at the people in your group, not at your object the whole time
- Speak loudly enough that the person in the back can hear you
- When you finish, ask: "Does anyone have questions?"

**After you speak:**
- Answer questions with complete sentences
- Sit down

This is the whole pattern. You will use this same pattern — stand, speak with structure, make eye contact, answer questions, sit down — for the rest of your life. The only thing that changes is how complex your speeches become.

### Part 3: Recitation — Learning to Deliver Words That Are Not Your Own

Recitation means memorizing a poem, a passage, or a speech and saying it aloud to an audience. This is one of the oldest forms of education. For thousands of years, before most people could read, knowledge was passed from person to person through recitation. (If you are studying 1.1.1, you learned about civilizations that preserved their stories this way.)

Why recitation matters:
- **It trains your memory.** Memorizing is a skill that gets stronger with practice.
- **It gives you beautiful language.** When you memorize a great poem, those words become part of you. You carry them forever.
- **It builds delivery confidence.** When you know exactly what you are going to say, you can focus entirely on how you say it — your voice, your pace, your expression, your eye contact.

**How to memorize a poem:**
1. Read it aloud five times, all the way through
2. Read the first two lines ten times. Then say them without looking
3. Add two more lines. Say all four without looking
4. Continue until the whole poem is memorized
5. Practice it aloud every day for a week before you recite it

**How to recite well:**
- Stand still and tall
- Look at your audience, not at the ceiling
- Speak the words as if you mean them — not in a sing-song voice, not rushed, not mumbled
- Pause at the end of important lines. Let the words land
- When you finish, stand still for one moment before sitting down. Do not rush away

### Part 4: Telling a Story

Storytelling is the most natural form of public speaking. Humans have been telling stories for tens of thousands of years — longer than we have been writing, farming, or building cities.

A good story has three parts:
1. **The beginning** — who, where, when. Set the scene. "Last summer, my family went to the lake, and something happened that I still think about."
2. **The middle** — what happened. This is where the action is. Something changed, something went wrong, something surprising occurred. "We were out in the canoe when we heard a sound we had never heard before."
3. **The end** — how it turned out, and what it meant. "It turned out to be a loon. I had never heard one before. Now every time I hear that sound, I think of that day."

When you tell a story:
- Use details. "A big bird" is less interesting than "a black-and-white bird with red eyes that made a sound like a person laughing."
- Change your voice for different parts. Quieter for suspense. Louder for excitement. Slower for important moments.
- Look at your audience. Their faces tell you if they are with you.

### Part 5: Getting Comfortable with Attention

The hardest part of public speaking is not the speaking. It is the attention. When everyone is looking at you, your body reacts. Your heart beats faster. Your hands might shake. Your mind might go blank. This is normal. It happens to almost everyone.

The solution is not to make the feeling go away. The solution is to feel it so many times that it stops being scary. This is called **exposure** — the more you do something that makes you nervous, the less nervous it makes you.

Here are ways to practice being the center of attention:
- Lead a prayer or grace before a meal
- Give a toast at a family dinner: "I want to say thank you to Mom for making this food"
- Announce the next activity to the group: "We are going to the park now"
- Read a passage aloud to the family
- Introduce yourself to a group of new people

Every time you do one of these things, the next time is a little easier. That is how courage works — not by being unafraid, but by doing the thing even when you are afraid, until the fear shrinks.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: The Weekly Recitation [Ongoing — Backbone of the Packet]
The student memorizes and recites one poem or passage per month. Begin with short poems (4-8 lines) and progress to longer ones as confidence builds. Each recitation is delivered to the family or study group with full performance: standing, eye contact, vocal expression. The mentor provides feedback on delivery, not just accuracy.

### Study 2: Show and Tell Practice [Ongoing — Weekly]
The student prepares and delivers a one-minute structured presentation at least once per week. The subject changes each time. The mentor and any audience members ask at least two questions afterward. The student answers in complete sentences.

### Study 3: Storytelling Sessions [Bi-Weekly]
The student tells a story — real or retold from a book — to the family or group. The focus is on structure (beginning, middle, end), detail, and vocal expression. After telling, the group discusses: What was the most interesting part? What detail helped you see it in your mind?

### Study 4: The Recording Exercise [Monthly]
Record the student delivering a presentation or recitation. Watch it together. The student identifies one thing they did well and one thing they want to improve. This builds the habit of self-assessment that will serve them through every tier.

### Study 5: The Real Audience Challenge [At Least Twice During the Packet]
The student delivers a recitation or presentation to an audience beyond the family — a group of friends, a community group, a church gathering, a homeschool co-op. The audience must include people who are not the student's immediate family. This is where the skill becomes real.

### Study 6: Comfort with Attention Exercises [Ongoing]
The student regularly takes on small public roles: leading grace, making announcements, introducing themselves to new people, ordering for themselves in restaurants. The mentor tracks frequency and notes growth in confidence over weeks and months.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Comfort with Attention
**Developing well:** The student stands up willingly, does not rush through material, does not look at the floor, and does not exhibit physical distress signals (shaking, crying, refusing). They may still feel nervous — that is fine. They do it anyway.

**Developing slowly:** The student refuses to stand, cries when asked to speak, or rushes through material so fast it is unintelligible. Physical avoidance behaviors (hiding behind a parent, turning away from the audience) persist.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the audience too large or too unfamiliar? Start smaller. Is the student being put on the spot without preparation? Always give them time to prepare. Are other children in the group being unkind about presentations? Address the audience behavior first.

### 2. Speech Structure
**Developing well:** Presentations and stories have a recognizable beginning, middle, and end. The student does not just start talking and trail off — they open, develop, and conclude.

**Developing slowly:** The student rambles without structure, jumps between topics, or stops abruptly without a conclusion.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you explicitly taught the three-part structure? Have you modeled it yourself? Use the prompt: "First tell me what your topic is. Then tell me the interesting things about it. Then tell me what you want us to remember."

### 3. Vocal Delivery
**Developing well:** The student speaks at a volume everyone can hear, at a pace that is neither rushed nor dragging, in complete sentences.

**Developing slowly:** The student mumbles, speaks too quickly to be understood, or speaks in fragments.

**Diagnostic questions:** Practice volume at home: "Say that again so the person in the back yard could hear you." Record the student and play it back — hearing their own quiet voice is often more motivating than being told to speak up.

### 4. Recitation Quality
**Developing well:** The student can deliver memorized material with expression — not in a monotone or sing-song voice, but as if the words mean something. Eye contact is maintained at least some of the time.

**Developing slowly:** The student recites in a flat or rushing voice, stares at a fixed point, or loses their place frequently.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student choosing material they connect with? A child who finds their poem boring will deliver it boringly. Is the student practicing daily? Memorization requires daily repetition for at least a week.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Forcing a Reluctant Child
If a child is genuinely frightened, forcing them to stand in front of a large group will make the fear worse, not better. Start with an audience of one (you). Then two. Then the family. Then a small group. Build gradually. The goal is exposure at a pace that stretches without breaking.

### Performing Only for Family
The family audience is the training ground. It is not the test. The student must eventually speak to people who are not their parents and siblings — a co-op group, a community gathering, extended family. Comfort that exists only in the living room is not transferable competence.

### Skipping Recitation
Recitation feels old-fashioned. It is. It also works. The child who memorizes and delivers poems builds memory, language, confidence, and delivery simultaneously. Do not skip it because it seems antiquated. Charlotte Mason made it central to education for good reason.

### Over-Coaching Delivery
At Stretching Branches, the priority is willingness and structure, not polish. A child who stands up, says what they planned to say, and sits down has succeeded — even if their delivery was imperfect. Perfectionism about delivery at this age creates the exact anxiety you are trying to prevent.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: A Time You Spoke in Front of People
**Strong:** Specific memory with sensory detail — what they felt, what happened, what they learned. Personal reflection on the experience.
**Weak:** "I gave a presentation." No detail, no feeling, no reflection.

### Prompt 2: Why People Get Nervous
**Strong:** Can describe physical sensations of nervousness and articulate that practice reduces fear. May reference their own experience.
**Weak:** "I don't know" or "Because it's scary" with no elaboration.

### Prompt 3: Recitation
**Strong:** Delivers the poem with expression and eye contact. Can discuss what the poem means in their own words.
**Weak:** Rushes through monotonically. Cannot explain what the poem is about.

### Prompt 4: Tell a Story
**Strong:** Clear beginning, middle, end. Uses specific sensory details. Voice changes for different parts of the story.
**Weak:** Disconnected events with no structure. "And then... and then... and then..." without a point.
